items.myth: A failed HTTP request (404/500) makes fetch reject.
items.reality: fetch resolves for any completed HTTP response regardless of status; only network-level failures reject. Check res.ok.
items.example: `await fetch('/missing')` for a 404 returns a normal `Response` with `status: 404, ok: false`. You won't notice unless you check — `await r.json()` may even succeed parsing an error payload.

items.reality: It does not; without an AbortController a slow request can hang indefinitely.
items.example: `await fetch('https://slow.example')` against a server that never responds will wait forever (or until the OS times out the TCP connection, often minutes). Always pair with `AbortController` + `setTimeout`.

items.myth: res.json() gives you the data synchronously.
items.reality: res.json() returns a Promise; you must await it to read the parsed body.
items.example:
```ts
const data = fetch(url).then(r => r.json())   // 'data' is a Promise, not the object
const data = await (await fetch(url)).json()    // both must be awaited
```
